seo.title: Phitsanulok Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Phitsanulok Province: Phra Buddha Chinnarat, Phu Hin Rong Kla National Park, phak bung loi fa street food, and the gateway to Sukhothai. Area: 10,816 km², population: 865,000.

overview: Phitsanulok is a lower northern province of Thailand straddling the Nan River, best known as home to the Phra Buddha Chinnarat — widely regarded as Thailand's most beautiful Buddha image after the Emerald Buddha. The provincial capital sits at a key rail and road junction, making it the natural gateway to the ancient kingdom of Sukhothai 60 km to the west. Beyond the city, the province extends into rugged highlands: Phu Hin Rong Kla National Park in the northeast preserves dense forest and the haunting ruins of a former communist insurgency stronghold. Phitsanulok's food scene has its own national celebrity — phak bung loi fa (flying morning glory), a dish tossed and caught through the air in a theatrical street-side performance that has become a pilgrimage for Thai foodies.
geography: Phitsanulok covers 10,816 km² in the lower northern region of Thailand, occupying a transitional zone between the northern mountains and the central plains. The Nan River bisects the city from north to south, historically enabling trade and earning Phitsanulok its nickname 'City of Two Rivers' (the Nan and the smaller Khwae Noi). The province's eastern flank rises steeply into the Phetchabun Range, reaching elevations above 1,700 metres at Phu Hin Rong Kla and the adjacent Thung Salaeng Luang National Park. The western lowlands transition toward the Sukhothai plain. The province shares a short border with Laos (Sainyabuli Province) to the northeast.
highlights: Phra Buddha Chinnarat at Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat — one of Thailand's most revered Buddha images, cast in 1357 and covered in gold
Gateway to Sukhothai — the 13th-century capital of the first Thai kingdom, just 60 km west with UNESCO World Heritage status
Phu Hin Rong Kla National Park — cool highland forest, communist history museums, and dramatic rock formations 130 km from the city
Phak bung loi fa (flying morning glory) — Phitsanulok's most famous culinary spectacle, performed at riverside restaurants since the 1960s
Nan River boat rides — passing stilted wooden houses, local markets, and working fishermen in the heart of the city
Sergeant Major Thawee Folk Museum — one of Thailand's finest provincial folk-life collections, housed across multiple buildings
Thung Salaeng Luang National Park — vast savanna grasslands and one of Thailand's most important wildlife corridors
Night market along the Nan River (Rim Nan) — lively riverside dining with fresh river fish, grilled meats, and local specialties

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool season with temperatures of 18–28°C, low humidity, and minimal rainfall. The most comfortable period for both city sightseeing and trekking in Phu Hin Rong Kla. Mornings can be genuinely cool in the highlands.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season with temperatures reaching 35–38°C in the city. March and April are dry; May brings the first rains. Songkran (April 13–15) is celebrated energetically along the Nan River waterfront.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Monsoon season. Waterfalls at Kaeng Sopha and inside the national parks are at their most impressive. Highland roads can be slippery; some trails close. Hotel rates drop noticeably.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: November to February is ideal for the city and national parks alike. Avoid April–May heat for hiking. The monsoon season rewards waterfall chasers but demands a 4WD vehicle for highland roads.

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat (Wat Yai)
topAttractions.description: Phitsanulok's most sacred site and one of the holiest temples in all of Thailand. The temple was founded in 1357 by King Lithai of Sukhothai and houses the Phra Buddha Chinnarat — a 375 cm seated Buddha image covered in gold paint, considered the most beautiful in Thailand after the Emerald Buddha. The surrounding wihan is richly ornamented with gilded lacquerwork and mother-of-pearl doors. A small museum on the grounds displays Buddha images and religious artefacts. Open daily 06:30–18:00.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 40 THB for the inner wihan (museum); the outer temple grounds are free

sources.sourceName: Wikipedia — Wat Phra Si Rattana Mahathat

topAttractions.name: Phu Hin Rong Kla National Park
topAttractions.description: A compelling combination of natural beauty and 20th-century history. The park's highland plateau (1,200–1,820 m) was the last stronghold of the Communist Party of Thailand from the 1960s until 1982, and the original headquarters, hospital, and printing presses are preserved as open-air museums. The landscape features dramatic Hin Rong Kla rock formations, Lom Sak Cliff with panoramic views, and the Lan Hin Pum stone field. Wildflowers carpet the grasslands from October to January. Located 130 km northeast of Phitsanulok city.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB adults / 100 THB children (foreigners); 40 THB / 20 THB (Thai nationals)

topAttractions.name: Sergeant Major Thawee Folk Museum
topAttractions.description: One of Thailand's most acclaimed provincial folk-life museums, assembled over decades by the eponymous retired soldier Sergeant Major Thawee Buranakhet. The multi-building complex displays traditional farming tools, household objects, textiles, and photographs documenting pre-modern life in the lower northern provinces, with informative English captions throughout. Directly across the road is the Buranathai Buddha Casting Foundry (free), where artisans cast large-scale reproductions of the Chinarat image. The adjacent Thai Bird Garden houses hornbills, silver pheasants, and other native species. Open daily 08:00–16:00.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB (museum); Foundry: free; Bird Garden: 50 THB

topAttractions.name: Thung Salaeng Luang National Park
topAttractions.description: Thailand's largest savanna grassland (the thung), spanning parts of Phitsanulok and Phetchabun provinces. The open grasslands draw gaur, banteng, sambar deer, elephants, and a rich array of birds including green peafowl. The Kaeng Sopha Waterfall — a wide, gentle cascade over a laterite shelf — is located within the park and is the most accessible scenic highlight, open daily 08:00–17:00. Best visited October to February when wildlife is active on the plains.

topAttractions.name: Nan River (Rim Nan Waterfront)
topAttractions.description: The Nan River flows through the heart of Phitsanulok city and its waterfront is the social spine of local life. Long-tail boat tours depart from the pier near Wat Yai, passing traditional stilted wooden homes, working fishermen, and river-side temples. The Rim Nan night market stretches along the eastern bank each evening with fresh river fish, grilled meats, and the local kway teow hoi khae (river mussel noodle soup). The boat pier near Naresuan Road is the main departure point.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free (riverfront access); boat tours from approximately 100 THB per person

topAttractions.name: Phak Bung Loi Fa Restaurants (Flying Morning Glory)
topAttractions.description: Phitsanulok's most theatrical dining experience and one of Thailand's most distinctive street-food spectacles. At dedicated riverside restaurants — most concentrated on Phuttha Bucha Road — a cook stir-fries morning glory (water spinach) in a blazing wok, then hurls the finished dish through the air to a waiter who catches it on a plate ten metres away. The tradition dates to the 1960s. The dish itself (phak bung fai daeng) is crisp, garlicky, and spiced with chilli — best enjoyed with khao man gai and cold beer. Evenings are the most atmospheric time to go.
topAttractions.entranceFee: No cover charge; dishes from 60–120 THB

localFood: Phak bung loi fa (flying morning glory) — stir-fried water spinach with garlic and chilli, theatrically tossed through the air and caught on a plate; Phitsanulok's signature spectacle since the 1960s
Kway teow hoi khae — rice noodle soup with river mussels (hoi khae), clear pork broth, and fresh herbs; the quintessential Phitsanulok street food found at the Rim Nan night market
Khao man gai — poached chicken over fragrant rice with clear broth and a ginger-chilli dipping sauce; ubiquitous for breakfast and lunch throughout the city
Kanom jeen nam ngiao — fermented rice noodles in a rich northern-style tomato and pork blood broth with dried chillis; common in the markets of Mueang district
Miang kham — a northern snack of wild betel leaves topped with shredded coconut, dried shrimp, ginger, lime, chilli, and palm sugar sauce; served as a street snack near Wat Yai
Pla ra pla pow — fermented fish sauce used as a seasoning base in local curries and dips; reflects the Nan River fishing culture that defines the province's culinary identity
culture: Phitsanulok occupies an important place in Thai history: it was the birthplace of King Naresuan the Great (1555), the warrior king who liberated Siam from Burmese rule. The provincial spirit is shaped by its dual identity — a forward-looking transport hub and a custodian of deep historical heritage. The Phra Buddha Chinnarat draws devout pilgrims from across Thailand year-round, making Wat Yai one of the busiest merit-making temples outside Bangkok. The annual Phitsanulok Candle Festival (July, coinciding with Khao Phansa) sees elaborately carved wax candles paraded through the city. The province also celebrates the memory of King Naresuan with a week-long festival in late January.
gettingThere: Phitsanulok is served by Phitsanulok Airport (PHS) with multiple daily flights to Bangkok (Don Mueang) on Nok Air and AirAsia, approximately 1 hour. By train from Bangkok's Hua Lamphong or Bang Sue Grand Station, express and special-express services take 5–6 hours on the Northern Line, stopping at Phitsanulok city-centre station — by far the most convenient rail arrival in upper Thailand. By bus from Bangkok Mo Chit Bus Terminal, the journey takes approximately 5.5–6 hours (VIP coaches from 350 THB). From Chiang Mai, buses run via Lampang and take 4–5 hours. Phitsanulok is the main bus junction for onward travel to Sukhothai (1 hour, 50 THB songthaew) and Loei.
gettingAround: Phitsanulok city centre is compact and walkable between Wat Yai, the Nan River waterfront, and the Sergeant Major Thawee Folk Museum. Tuk-tuks are the main local transport (40–80 THB for short hops); motorbike taxis cover the same routes for slightly less. City buses #1 and #8 run along the main arteries every 15 minutes (10 THB flat fare) with stops near the Asia Hotel on Akathotsarot Road. Hiring a songthaew or renting a motorbike (200–300 THB/day from shops near the train station) is essential for reaching Phu Hin Rong Kla or Thung Salaeng Luang. A rented car (800–1,000 THB/day) is recommended for the mountain roads to the national parks, particularly in the wet season.
travelTips: Visit Wat Yai early morning (06:30–08:00) before tour groups arrive — the golden Chinnarat image in low morning light is breathtaking and the atmosphere is genuinely devotional
Dress modestly at all temples — shoulders and knees covered; sarongs are available to borrow at Wat Yai's entrance gate
For phak bung loi fa, arrive at riverside restaurants by 18:00 to secure a good viewing spot; the flying performance happens as dishes are ordered
Phu Hin Rong Kla is best in Oct–Jan for wildflowers and cool temperatures; the road (Highway 2331) is a steep 130 km drive — allow 2.5 hours each way from the city
The train is the best way to arrive: Phitsanulok station is 500 m from Wat Yai and walking distance to most guesthouses
Rent a motorbike or hire a driver for Thung Salaeng Luang — the grasslands are 100 km from the city and no public transport runs that route
The Rim Nan night market is liveliest Thursday to Sunday — arrive by 18:30 for the best selection of fresh river fish grilled to order
Phitsanulok is a comfortable overnight base for visiting Sukhothai: the UNESCO ruins open at 06:00, and an early songthaew (first departure ~07:00) gets you there before the heat builds
